topic: Someone discovers their recipe has become famous under another name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the blunt force of a thrown brick. "The Crimson Swirl: A Culinary Revelation." My stomach clenched, a tight, unpleasant knot forming beneath my ribs. I clicked it open, bracing myself for the inevitable. The headline featured a glossy photograph of a dish I had painstakingly perfected in my grandmother’s kitchen—a simple, comforting lavender-infused shortbread. But the accompanying text spoke of 'revolutionary technique' and 'unparalleled flavor complexity.' My hands, usually steady when kneading dough, began to tremble as I reread the breathless prose. I scrolled down, my eyes darting over the reviews, each glowing endorsement a fresh wave of sourness washing over me. I felt a profound sense of being utterly bypassed, like a quiet, well-made instrument being replaced by a blaring, unfamiliar fanfare. I deleted the email immediately, the screen burning my retinas, and retreated to the kitchen, the scent of stale flour suddenly revolting.

[story 2]

I found the article tucked between spam and urgent work notifications. The name attached to the recipe—'The Alchemist's Delight'—was dripping with undeserved adoration. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. I stared at the picture: my slightly lopsided, home-baked apple pie, elevated to some sort of gastronomic high art. A wave of heat rose in my cheeks, and I instinctively pulled my cardigan tighter around myself, as if trying to physically shield myself from the glare of the praise. "This isn't mine," I muttered to the empty room, the words tasting like ash. I began pacing the length of my small apartment, my movements jerky and agitated. Every time I touched the counter where I kept my handwritten notes, a sharp, unpleasant recoil shot up my arm. It felt like watching someone else wear my face, only to claim the credit for something I had nurtured in secret, a bitter taste coating the back of my throat.

[story 3]

The notification pinged, demanding attention. It was a link to a trending food blog, featuring a photograph of a dish that was undeniably mine. The accompanying caption hailed the creation as 'the benchmark of modern pastry.' A strange, cold sensation settled in my chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. I leaned back in my chair, my gaze fixed on the screen, unable to look away from the glossy, professional presentation. It was the exact same recipe—the one I spent three years refining, the one that felt like an extension of my own soul. Now, it belonged to some stranger, someone who had somehow repackaged my honest effort into something glittering and commercially viable. I slowly pushed my chair back, the scrape of the legs on the hardwood floor sounding jarringly loud in the silence. I walked to the window, watching the ordinary street below, feeling a deep, churning resentment bubble up, thick and heavy, demanding release.
